entries.author: Jean Rigaud / Johannes Rigaldi (d. 1323)
entries.title: De expositione missae
entries.remarks: See Roest 364-65: MS Paris, Bibliothèque Nationale lat. 3150, which contains the Compendium Pauperis by the early fourteenth-century French friar Jean Rigaud, repeatedly states that this Franciscan also produced an Expositio Missae: ‘In fine autem tractatus seu lecturae quam ordinavi De Expositione Missae invenies xxiiii mirabilia per ordinem in hoc sacramento’ (f. 79r); ‘Nota quod vestis episcopalis et sacerdotalis plenius indicant quales esse debent qui hoc sacramentum ministrant. Et, licet de hoc dictum sit in tractatu deordinato Super missam, tamen ut aliquid hic dicatur . . .’ (f. 79v); ‘Circa missae officium, licet de hoc impletius dicatur in tractatu quem feci De Expositione Missae, aliqua sunt sub brevitate recitanda’ (f. 80r). All my information on this is based on the remarks by Valois, ‘Jean Rigaud, frère mineur’, 297–298, who suggests that maybe the anonymous De Significationibus Missae found in MS Dresden, Sächsische Landesbibliothek Theol. A 55 ff. 123–129 should be ascribed to Jean Rigaud (this same manuscript contains Jean’s Formula Confessionum on ff. 344–364. Biography.

entries.author: Nicholas Andreas Theatinus
entries.title: Informatio electorum/clericorum (+ cum commento)
entries.remarks: See Franz 607. Sources are Decretum, Decretals, and Durand. For further MSS and biography, see: Bislang nur in den Codices Uppsala, UB, C 588, 40r–60v (Uppsala 6, 71), und Wien, Schottenstift, Archiv, Hs. 289, 1r–30v (Hübl, 317f. Nr. 293), nachgewiesen. Zu dem nur durch diesen, 1314–1316 in Avignon entstandenen Text bekannten Kanonisten aus Chieti vgl. Schulte 2, 398 Nr. 247. Der Autor ist möglicherweise mit dem gleichnamigen Kanoniker von St. Peter in Rom und späteren Bischof von Catanzaro († ca. 1369) identisch, dazu Eubel, 181.

entries.author: Martinus lector
entries.title: Expositio missae (Melk, BS Cod. 341, 109ra-158va)
entries.remarks: Melk, Benediktinerstift Cod. 341, 109ra-158va; Klagenfurt, Universitätsbibliothek (früher: Studienbibliothek), Pap.-Hs. 90, ff 24r-54r [dated 1397]; Klosterneuburg, Bibl. d. Chorherrenstifts 359, f. 2; Klosterneuburg, Bibl. d. Chorherrenstifts 339, f. 2 [??]; Prague N. 18 (1542) [15^1], 1a-76b; Graz, UB 678, f. 83; Anbach, SB Ms. lat. 93, f. 139ra. See Mohan, G.E., Initia operum Franciscalium (XIII-XV S.) p. 193, in: Franciscan Studies 37 (1977) 179-375; Rothschild, J.-P., Bibliographie annuelle du Moyen Age tardif, t. 5, 1995, n° 2730. Weitere Literatur: Hugo Dausend: Literarische Eigenart und Abfassungsverhältnisse der 'Expositio divini officii missae' des Franziskanerlektors Martin von Wien (Cod. Vindobon. 350), in: Kirchengeschichtliche Studien P. Michael Bihl O.F.M. als Ehrengabe dargeboten. Hrsg. von Ignatius-Maria Freudenreich. Kolmar 1941, 145-155 , 145–155). Die Expositio bei Franz, Messe als anonymes Werk angesehen, bei Mohan 193* unter Verweis auf Klagenfurt, UB, Pap. 90, 23v–54v (datiert 1397) und Dausend einem Martinus lector Viennae zugeschrieben. [But in Melk 341 it appears in a part completed in 1366!]. Cf. Roest, Franciscan Literature, p. 364. [Perhaps Melk, Benediktinerstift, Cod. 341 (377, G 43). This treatise draws long extracts from Innocent, treats the Our Father, Purgatory, the Eucharist, pericula contingentibus

entries.author: Heinric von Freimar
entries.title: Expositio missae (Sic nos existimet)
entries.remarks: Henricus de Frimaria (b. 1245) was a German scholar from Gotha, a prominent figure within the Augustinian order, and nominated provincial leader of the Augustinians of Thuringia and Saxony in 1315. In 1317, he became Magister regens of the Augustinian studium in Erfurt, where he was active for over 20 years until his death in 1340. For a biographical overview see Adolar Zumkeller, Heinrich von Friemar der Ältere, in: Lexikon des Mittelalters, vol. 4, Munich, Zürich 1989, col. 2091, as well as Robert Glenn Warnock, Heinrich von Friemar d.Ä., in: Die deutsche Literatur des Mittelalters. Verfasserlexikon, vol. 11, Berlin, New York 2004, cols. 623–624. Clm 14709, 104r-124r [Inc: Sic nos existimet]; Biblioteka Jagiellońska, BJ Rkp. 1394 III t. 1, 1r; Erfurt, Wissenschaftl. entries.author: Guilelmus de Gouda (Willem Tergouw)
entries.title: Expositio mysteriorum missae et verus modus rite celebrandi / Tractatus de expositione misse et verus modus rite celebrandi
entries.remarks:
See Roest, 365 ff for bibliography. For a more or less complete overview of the various editions, see especially P. Schlager, ‘Ueber die Meßerklärung des Franziskaners Wilhelm von Gouda’, FrSt 6 (1919), 323–336; B. De
Troeyer, ‘Guillaume de Gauda’, DSpir VI, 1208–9; Idem, Bio-bibliographia Franciscana Neerlandica, Ante Saec. XVI I, 124–7; Idem, ‘Bio-bibliografie van de minderbroeders vóór het jaar 1500. Voorstudies (nieuwe reeks) VII. Guilelmus de Gouda’, Franciscana 29 (1974), 21–26. There were at least 25 editions until 1504. See especially the studies of De Troeyer. [In this concise work, the author intended first of all to provide parish priests and other simple priests with a manual enabling them to perform the liturgy of the Mass correctly, to properly understand all elements of it, and to perform the Mass with the right mental disposition (‘digne conficere et fructuose immolare sacramentum eucharistie’). The work also insists on a proper instruction of the faithfull (catechistic impulse), so that they will gain a proper understanding of the mysteries of Christ’s life and sacrifice, which are represented allegorically by the various elements of the Mass. The work therefore can be seen in the context of the Observant program of religious reform. The work exhibits some local usages in the Cologne area during the closing decades of the fifteenth century, but on the whole follows faithfully the major Mass expositions of the later Middle Ages (a.o. John Beleth, Sicard of Cremona, works of Innocent III, Hugh of St. Cher’s Speculum Ecclesiae (d. 1263), Guillelmus Durandus, and the Opusculum super Missam of the Franciscan friar William of Middletown (d. c. 1257). In various editions, the Expositio is followed by a series of ‘Orationes devotissime ante et post missam dicende.’]

entries.author: Josse Clichtove

entries.remarks: Contains commentary on the hours (100), on sequences, hymns, on the mass (115). Gloss format: Gloria, Creed, Canon. For Clichtove, the works of Jean-Pierre Massaut are indispensable. See Jean-Pierre Massaut, Josse Clichtove: l’humanisme et la réforme du clergé, 2 vols. (Paris: Les Belles lettres, 1968); idem, Critique et tradition à la veille de la Réforme en France (Paris: Vrin, 1974); and for an updated bibliography of secondary literature, idem, ‘Clichtove (Josse)’, in Colette Nativel (ed.), Centuriae Latinae II: cent une figures humanistes de la Renaissance aux Lumières (Geneva: Droz, 2006), 221–25.

entries.author: Franciscus Tittelmans

entries.remarks: Tractatus de Expositione Mysteriorum Missae. Sacri Canonis Missae Duplex Expositio. Tractatus Sanctarum Meditationum (Antwerp, 1528); Espositione del R.P. Frate Francesco Titelmano dei misteri e cerimonie, le quale si osservano nel santissimo sacrificio della messa, secondo l’ordine delli nostri santi e antichi Padri (Venice, 1548). Written during Titelman’s Observant years as a theologian at Louvain, the Tractatus was well received among the early Capuchins. For that reason parts of the preface and fragments of the text (distinctions three, four, seven, and the conclusion) of the 1548 Italian edition can be found in: I Frati Cappuccini III/1, 250–264. See Roest.

entries.author: Jacob Janssonius
entries.title: In Sacrum Missae Canonem quo Romana utitur Ecclesia Expositio
entries.remarks: Long preface on the spiritual nature of sacrifice. W/ a short preface invoking S Bernard, it is a Commentary on the Offertory, Canon, and Communion. Remarks on each word or phrase are rich: doctrinal, ascetical, moral, patristic (esp. Irenaeus, Augustine, Ambrose, Cyril, Bede, Dionysius, Bernard) and scriptural. No division and no citation of the previous tradition, almost no historical remarks. Nothing about ceremonial (except at consecration) or signs of the cross. Little polemic, not much on transubstantiation. The consecration is a commemoration of his death but not allegorically. Pg 161: signs of the cross at Per ipsum

entries.author: Jacques de Bay
entries.title: De Venerabili Eucharistiae Sacramento et Sacrificio
entries.remarks:
Counter-reform text. Dedicated to St. Francis de Sales! Written for seminarians in Louvain training to preach to Calvinists. 
Book 3, c. 31: De vestibus sacris: ad Christi passionem representandam simulque ad ministri doctrinam; c. 32: On places; c. 33: actions (including defense of signs of the cross, pg 354); sources mostly patristic, esp Dionysius and Basil; c. 34: Compendiosa expositio formulae missae, pp. 354-366 (Rupert, Innocent, Ambrose, Durandus, Strabo, Alcuin, Amalar, Hugo): extra focus on history; c. 35-39: Canonis missae paraphrasis; c. 40: Our Father

entries.remarks: Moral theology. pp. 824 ff: Dubium XX: Classification of literature on the Mass: his is moral theology. He omits discussion of single ceremonies, only focusing on a defense of the Dominican manner of communing. Moral questions on preparation for Mass, ommission of words, etc. Dubium XXI: De iis quae in celebratione missae aguntur.

entries.author: B. Gavanti († 1638), C. Merati († l744), M. Quarti († after 1650), J. Bona († 1674), and J. Catalani († 1764)
entries.title: Mystica expositio missae (Thesaurus sac. rit, Pars II, tit. XVI)
entries.remarks: He cites all sources, draws from the whole tradition: Florus, Alcuin, Germanus, Rupert, Honorius, Hugo, Stephen of Liege, Alexander, Innocent, Micrologus, Durandus, Aquinas, Radulph, Melchior, Titelman, Biel, Princess Maria, etc.

entries.author: Hieronymus Emser
entries.title: Missae Christianorum contra Lutheranam missandi formulã Assertio
entries.remarks: Goes through each part of the mass justifying it, often citing Rabanus, Isidore, Dionysius, and Durandus. Lists Luther's proposed changes to the Mass and refutes them.

entries.author: John Cochlaeus
entries.title: Sacerdotii Ac Sacrificii Novae Legis Defensio, Aduersus Vuolfgangi Musculi
entries.remarks: Defends Biel on the things necessary and congruent for Mass, explains transubstantiation, comments canon. He defends prayers of Canon by appeal to Biel and Albert. On pg #82, on the other ceremonies of the canon, he lists the authorities: Albert, Thomas, Bonaventure, Alexander, Innocent, Hugo, Thomas Vualden Anglus, Gabriel Biel Germanus, Iudocus Clichtoveus Gallus, et haereticorum Censor Alfonsus Hispanus, **He doesn't use allegory, just uses the authors as authorities.

entries.author: Anonymous
entries.title: Exegesis Brevis in missae canonem
entries.remarks: Counter-reform tract. Mass is Hebrew word. Non-allegorical / patristic. Same volume contains Hildebert's Versus

entries.author: Conradus Brunus
entries.title: De caeremoniis libri sex
entries.remarks: Dedication offers religious anthropology. Book 2, chapter 1: On the mysteries of the Mass. Cites Clichtove. Largely concerned to defend the historicity and apostolicity of the rites: relies heavily on Dionysius, the dogma of the Mass as sacrifice, prayer for the dead. For the rest, they are 'non sine magnorum mysteriorum significatione," but he refers us to other books on the subject. Counter-reform text. Cites Innocent III a few times but no one else.

Tome I: De antiquis sacramentorum ritibus: Tome II: Benedictiones sacrae: Tome III: De variis ad ecclesiasticam disciplinam pertinentibus ritibus: Tome IV: Divina officia=liturgy of the hours and the liturgical year. Cf. A. G. Martimort, La Documentation liturgique de Dom Edmond Martine: Etude codicologique, SeT 279 (Vatican 1978); Supplement Ecclesia Orans 3 (1986) 81-105.
